html:
V-Technology: an FPD label masking an AI packaging equipment option

V-Technology at ¥5,540 is no longer the dirt-cheap net-cash display cyclical it looked like a few months ago. The stock has moved, and the April 30 guide-down exposed a real flaw: deliveries slipped in semiconductor, photomask, and China FPD equipment. But the latest numbers also make the new thesis cleaner. This is not primarily a Gen 8.6 OLED photomask rebound story now. It is a small Japanese FPD equipment company being slowly re-labeled as an advanced packaging, DI exposure, wafer inspection, and photomask equipment supplier.

The May 12 results show FY3/26 sales of ¥53.0 billion, operating profit of ¥3.77 billion, net income of ¥2.30 billion, and operating cash flow of ¥5.75 billion. Management guides FY3/27 to ¥60.0 billion revenue, ¥5.5 billion operating profit, and ¥3.0 billion net income, or ¥317 EPS. At today’s price, that is about 17.5x forward earnings and roughly 8x EV/EBIT after net cash. This is not a screen-cheap trailing multiple story. The mispricing is that the market is applying a cyclical display-equipment multiple to a company whose order book and product roadmap are increasingly tied to AI semiconductor packaging.

The inflection is visible in orders. FY3/26 orders rose to ¥56.3 billion and backlog ended at ¥47.0 billion, with a very large ¥32.7 billion Q4 order intake. Semiconductor and photomask orders were ¥23.4 billion for the year, up from ¥16.6 billion. More important, advanced packaging and PCB orders jumped to ¥7.75 billion, with ¥6.45 billion arriving in Q4 alone. That is the number that changes the narrative. A one-quarter spike does not prove a new secular business, but it does prove that the AI packaging exposure is no longer just slideware.

The business transition is unusually easy to miss because V-Tech still looks like an old display name. FPD remains the larger revenue bucket, and FY3/26 FPD operating profit carried the year. But management’s own strategic framing is “from FPD to semiconductors,” with the semiconductor and photomask segment expected to drive the FY3/27 margin improvement. The company is targeting high-marginal-profit semiconductor and photomask mix, using legacy display engineering capacity in adjacent precision equipment markets. That is the kind of wrong-label setup that can work in Japan small caps: the historical customer base is cyclical, but the core competence is precision exposure, inspection, repair, and test.

The advanced packaging angle is credible. V-Tech’s presentation explicitly maps its tools into AI package substrates, interposers, RDL, glass substrate possibilities, chiplet packaging, CoWoS-style roadmaps, and package inspection. Its LAMBDI direct imaging exposure tool received a NEDO order for an advanced packaging application at L/S = 0.5 micrometer class, and the company says its DI exposure tool has No. 1 share in China. Its LIBRA tester targets ultra-fine package substrate and interposer electrical inspection at pitches where conventional inspection struggles. These are not commodity FPD tools being dressed up with AI language. They sit in real bottlenecks created by larger AI packages, finer wiring, and higher substrate complexity.

The balance sheet gives time, but it is not the thesis by itself. Cash and deposits were ¥29.5 billion at March 2026, against about ¥21.1 billion of interest-bearing debt, leaving roughly ¥8.4 billion of net cash. Equity is ¥36.2 billion, or ¥3,826 per share. At ¥5,540, the stock is about 1.45x book. That is enough asset backing to matter, but not enough to justify a lazy “net cash cheap” call. The return on that capital is what matters. FY3/27 guided ROE is only 8.0%. The upside requires management to convert the semiconductor and advanced packaging pipeline into a structurally higher operating margin, not merely hoard cash and pay a ¥80 dividend.

The bear case is straightforward. V-Tech missed its prior plan, product warranty costs rose, Chinese FPD localization is pressuring some equipment categories, and advanced packaging deliveries can slip because these are customer-specific, qualification-heavy tools. The April 30 revision was not cosmetic. FY3/26 operating profit was cut from ¥4.5 billion to ¥3.5 billion before the final result came in at ¥3.77 billion. If FY3/27 also slips, the market will treat the Q4 order surge as timing noise and push the stock back toward book value.

But the setup is attractive because the market does not need to believe the full FY3/29 target for the stock to work. Management still shows a FY3/29 ambition of ¥100 billion revenue and ¥20 billion operating profit. That target is aggressive, but even partial success changes valuation. If FY3/28 revenue reaches ¥75 billion with a 12 percent operating margin, V-Tech would earn around ¥9 billion of EBIT. Put an 8x EV/EBIT multiple on that, add net cash, and the equity value is around ¥80 billion, or roughly ¥8,500 per share. That is not heroic if advanced packaging orders continue to compound.

The true bull case is a re-labeling. If investors stop seeing V-Tech as a China FPD capex vendor and start seeing it as a small-cap Japanese AI packaging equipment supplier with exposure to DI exposure, O/S test, wafer inspection, photomask, and process-adjacent tools, the multiple changes before peak earnings arrive. Japanese semiconductor equipment peers with credible AI supply-chain exposure do not trade at single-digit forward EV/EBIT when orders are accelerating. V-Tech does because its history is messy and the segment names still look old.

The next two quarters matter. Confirmation is not a press release, it is another step-up in semiconductor and advanced packaging backlog, lower warranty drag, and evidence that Q4 orders convert into revenue without another installation delay. A buyback would help, but it is secondary. The main catalyst is proof that the FY3/27 ¥5.5 billion operating profit guide is conservative, not another number waiting to be revised down.

At the May 18, 2026 close of ¥5,540, my targets are:

Bear: ¥3,700, down 33 percent. FY3/27 misses, advanced packaging remains lumpy, FPD margins normalize lower, and the stock falls toward book value with a modest premium for cash and backlog.

Base: ¥8,800, up 59 percent. FY3/27 guidance is met, FY3/28 operating profit power moves toward ¥8-9 billion, and the market prices V-Tech at 8-9x EV/EBIT as a mixed FPD and semiconductor equipment company.

Bull: ¥16,000, up 189 percent. Advanced packaging becomes the driver, semiconductor and photomask mix lifts margins materially, FY3/29 operating profit reaches ¥14-16 billion, and the stock is re-rated as an overlooked AI packaging equipment supplier rather than a stale display cyclical.
